title: Suhrawardi S Creed Of The Sages
author: John Walbridge

body.text:
CHAPTER 5 Suhrawardī’s Creed of the Sages
John Walbridge At the end of Henry Corbin’sedition of al-Suhrawardī’s Philosophy of
Illumination (Ḥikmat al-ishrāq), there is alittle text entitled “The Creed of the
Sages” (Iʿtiqād al-ḥukamāʾ), to use the title given in al-Shahrazūrī’sbibliography of al-Suhrawardī’sworks.1 There are anumber of variations of the title:
Risāla fī iʿtiqād al-ḥukamāʾ in Qom, Maʿṣūmiyya 473; Tehran, Majlis 630
and 4903; and Tehran, Tehran University 1792;
Risālat al-iʿtiqād and Risālat al-iʿtiqādāt in Tehran, Majlis 1219;
ʿAqāʾid al-ḥukamāʾ (“Doctrines of the Sages”) in the important early
manuscript Tehran, Millī 2412 (992), as well as in Istanbul, Atatürk,
Ergin 1313;
Risāla fī tawjīh kalām al-ḥukamāʾ (“Essay on the Implication of the Sages’
Statements”) in Istanbul, Baǧdatlı Vehbi 2023;
Risāla fī bayān muʿtaqad al-mutaʿallim min ahl al-ḥaqīqa (“An Essay in
Explanation of What Is Believed by the One Taught by the People of
Reality”) in Istanbul, Karaçelebizade 346.

While it is by no means clear that al-Suhrawardī himself gave atitle to the
work—he does not himself name the work in the body of the text, referring
to it only as “this book” (hādhā al-kitāb)—Iʿtiqād al-ḥukamāʾ is appropriate.

The work is abrief summary of those areas of philosophy relevant to religious
doctrines.
Continued from the source text...

At anumber of points he introduces atopic with yaʿtaqidūna, “they
hold the belief,” aterm with specific reference to adherence to areligious
creed.

In the introduction he identifies those he is discussing as al-ḥukamāʾ
al-muta⁠ʾallihīn, “the divine sages,” aterm that he also uses in The Philosophy
of Illumination for the theistic ancient philosophers and in particular Plato.
Continued from the source text...

Among later Islamic philosophers the term ḥukamāʾ, which was used in the
Qurʾān, largely supplants the Greek loan word falāsifa, which by that time had
acquired an unpleasant connotation of free-thinking and atheism.
1 In Henry Corbin, ed., Oeuvres philosophiques et mystiques/Majmūʿa-i Muṣannafāt-i Shaykh-i
Ishrāq, vol. 2, pp. 261–271. © koninklijke brill nv, leiden, ���8 | doi ��.��63/9789004358393_006 Suhrawardī ’ s Creed of the Sages 67 Manuscripts
When Corbin edited The Creed of the Sages, he used three manuscripts, two
in Tehran and one in Paris.

As the edition was going to press, afourth turned
up, leading him to comment, “It is acurious fact of experience that as soon as
one publishes the rare or unique manuscripts of an unedited text, other manuscripts will unexpectedly appear!”2 Iknow of six more manuscripts that have
come to light since, though on the face of it Corbin seems to have found the
most important.
Continued from the source text...

All
are in majmūʿas, collections of shorter texts, the contents of which are important for understanding the transmission and circulation of this work.

Tehran, Millī Persian 2412/2, pp. 82–94, 9.5 × 19 cm, 264 ff., 19 ll.,
naskh, copied by Muḥammad b. ʿAlī al-Dāmghānī Jājarmī in 654/1256–659/1261,
“T” in Corbin’sedition.3 This is the second most important al-Suhrawardī manuscript, rivalled only by Istanbul, Ragip Paşa 1480, the great fourteenth century
compilation of his Arabic works, which does not include Iʿtiqād al-Ḥukamāʾ.
Continued from the source text...

Its
importance lies in its early date and the fact that manuscripts of those works
of al-Suhrawardī that it contains tend to be quite rare.

It was Seyyed Hossein
Nasr’ssource for most of the shorter Persian works that he edited in the third
volume of al-Suhrawardī’scollected works. 2 Henry Corbin, in Oeuvres, vol. 2, French p. 84.
3 Sayyid ʿAbd Allāh Anwār, Fihrist-i Nusakh-i Khaṭṭī-i Kitābkhāna-i Millī, vol. 5, no. 536–55.
Continued from the source text...

Muṣṭafā Dirāyatī, Fankhā: Fihristagān-i
nuskha-hā-yi khaṭṭī-i Īrān, vol. 4, p. 453.

Aḥmad Munzawī, Fihrist-i nuskhahā-i khaṭṭī-yi fārsī, vol. 2/1, pp. 1015–16, no. 9312; p. 1179, no. 11023;
p. 1271, no. 11929; p. 1287, no. 12077; p. 1344, no. 12720; p. 1430, no. 13703.
Continued from the source text...

There
is are microfilms in the Tehran University Library: F1136, F3059, F4619; see MuḥammadTaqī Dānish-Pazhūh, Fihrist-i Mīkrūfīlmhā-yi Kitābkhāna-i Markazī-yi Dānishgāh-i Tihrān,
[Vol. 1.], 1:566–67; vol. 2, p. 51; vol. 3, p. 80.

The late Hossein Ziai probably had amicrofilm, in which case the microfilm may be in the UCLA library, though Iwas not able to
locate it.
Continued from the source text...
